county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rankin County formed 1828 from Hinds County. County seat is Brandon. Part of the Jackson metropolitan area, directly east of the capital. Named for Christopher Rankin, Mississippi congressman.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rankin County (formed 1828 from Hinds) has Brandon as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1828 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Rankin County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. Rankin County borders Hinds County and is part of the Jackson metropolitan area. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The county had a mixed economy of cotton and small farming. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Jackson sub-district cover Rankin County. FamilySearch has digitized Rankin County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s document early families. The Rankin County Historical Society maintains local collections. Early settlers came from Georgia, the Carolinas, and Virginia.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Rankin County deed books from 1828; Chancery Clerk in Brandon maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Scott County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Forest. Adjacent to the Choctaw reservation with a significant Choctaw community. Named for General Abram M. Scott.
county_metadata.research_tips: Scott County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Forest as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Scott County records. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. Scott County is adjacent to the Choctaw reservation and has a Choctaw community -- tribal records are at NARA and the Choctaw tribal offices. The county had a mixed economy of farming and timber. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Scott County clerk records. Baptist church records from the 1830s are important sources. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding families. The county's early settlers came via the Natchez Trace.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Scott County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Forest maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sharkey County formed 1876 from Issaquena, Warren, and Washington counties. County seat is Rolling Fork, birthplace of Muddy Waters. A Delta county. Named for William L. Sharkey, provisional governor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sharkey County (formed 1876 from Issaquena, Warren, and Washington) has Rolling Fork as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1876. For pre-1876 records, check Warren County (Vicksburg), Washington County (Greenville), and Issaquena County (Mayersville). Sharkey County is a Delta county with a large plantation economy and predominantly African American population. MDAH has microfilm of Sharkey County records. The 1927 Great Flood devastated the county. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Delta are essential. BLM GLO land patents document early entries. FamilySearch has some Sharkey County records. Teddy Roosevelt's famous 1902 bear hunt occurred in Sharkey County. Levee board records document families along the Mississippi River.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Sharkey County deed books from 1876; Chancery Clerk in Rolling Fork maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Simpson County formed 1824 from Copiah County. County seat is Mendenhall. Located in south-central Mississippi. Named for Josiah Simpson, early Mississippi jurist.
county_metadata.research_tips: Simpson County (formed 1824 from Copiah) has Mendenhall as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1824 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Simpson County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. BLM GLO federal land patents document original entries. Simpson County is in south-central Mississippi with a farming and timber economy. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. FamilySearch has digitized Simpson County clerk records. Baptist church records from the 1830s are important sources. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding families. The D'Lo Water Park on Strong River reflects the county's rural heritage.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Simpson County deed books from 1824; Chancery Clerk in Mendenhall maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Smith County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Raleigh. Burned county: 1893 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Located in the Piney Woods of south-central Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Smith County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) is a burned county due to the 1893 courthouse fire. County seat is Raleigh. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and post-fire records. For the burned period (1833-1893), use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm of any surviving fragments, and census records from 1840 forward. Smith County is in the Piney Woods with a timber and subsistence farming economy. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. Church records from Baptist congregations supplement the burned civil records. FamilySearch has some Smith County records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps. The Sullivan Hollow community in Smith County has distinctive Scots-Irish heritage documented in local histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Smith County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Raleigh maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1893 courthouse fire destroyed early records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Stone County formed 1916 from Harrison County. County seat is Wiggins. Located in the Piney Woods of south Mississippi. Named for John M. Stone, governor of Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stone County (formed 1916 from Harrison) has Wiggins as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1916. For pre-1916 records, check Harrison County (Gulfport). MDAH has microfilm of Stone County records. Stone County is in the Piney Woods with a timber economy. The county was the site of significant timber operations by the Dantzler Lumber Company. FamilySearch has some Stone County records. Baptist church records are the primary denominational source for vital events. Tax rolls at MDAH document landholding families. Census records from 1920 forward capture the new county; earlier data is under Harrison County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Stone County deed books from 1916; Chancery Clerk in Wiggins maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sunflower County formed 1844 from Bolivar County. County seat is Indianola. A Delta county. Home to Parchman Farm (Mississippi State Penitentiary). Fannie Lou Hamer's civil rights activism centered in Ruleville. Named for the Sunflower River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sunflower County (formed 1844 from Bolivar) has Indianola as county seat, with Ruleville as another significant town. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1844 and chancery court records. Sunflower County is a Delta county with an overwhelmingly African American population historically tied to plantation agriculture. MDAH has microfilm of Sunflower County records. Fannie Lou Hamer, civil rights leader, was from Ruleville -- civil rights records are at MDAH and Tougaloo College. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Delta are essential. Parchman Farm (Mississippi State Penitentiary) is in Sunflower County -- prison records at MDAH document inmates. BLM GLO land patents document early entries. FamilySearch has digitized Sunflower County clerk records. The 1927 Great Flood impacted the county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Sunflower County deed books from 1844; Chancery Clerk in Indianola maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tallahatchie County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Charleston, with two judicial districts (Charleston and Sumner). Site of the 1955 Emmett Till murder trial. Named for the Tallahatchie River (Choctaw for 'rock of waters').
county_metadata.research_tips: Tallahatchie County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Charleston as county seat, with Sumner and Webb as the other major towns (two judicial districts). The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Tallahatchie County records. The county is best known as the site of the 1955 Emmett Till murder and trial in Sumner. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. The county had a large enslaved population in the Delta portion. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Tallahatchie County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s-1840s document early families. Tax rolls at MDAH trace landholding patterns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Tallahatchie County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Charleston maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tate County formed 1873 from DeSoto, Marshall, and Tunica counties. County seat is Senatobia. Located in northwest Mississippi. Home to Northwest Mississippi Community College. Named for a local family.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tate County (formed 1873 from DeSoto, Marshall, and Tunica) has Senatobia as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1873. For pre-1873 records, check DeSoto County (Hernando), Marshall County (Holly Springs -- note Civil War damage), and Tunica County (Tunica). MDAH has microfilm of Tate County records. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The county had a mixed economy of cotton and general farming. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Holly Springs sub-district cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Tate County clerk records. Northwest Mississippi Community College (1927) records may be useful. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1840s-1870s document families in the area before county formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Tate County deed books from 1873; Chancery Clerk in Senatobia maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Tate County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH

source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1873; original deed books held by Chancery Clerk in False

source_profiles.source_name: Tate County Chancery Court Records (Probate) -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Tate County Chancery Clerk, False / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mississippi uses Chancery Court for probate (unique system -- no separate probate court). Wills, estates, guardianships, and land partitions for Tate County from 1873; MDAH has microfilm of chancery minutes and estate files

source_profiles.source_name: Tate County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Tate County from 1880 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; the 1890 census was destroyed nationally

source_profiles.source_name: Tate County Enslaved Persons and Plantation Records -- MDAH / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tate County had a very large enslaved population. MDAH holds plantation records, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name, slave schedules (1850, 1860), and slaveholder correspondence. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA (M826, M1907) document formerly enslaved families post-war

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tippah County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Ripley. Located in northeast Mississippi hill country. The Faulkner family lived in Ripley before moving to Oxford. Named for a Chickasaw word.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tippah County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Ripley as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Tippah County land records, chancery minutes, and marriage books. Tippah County is in the hill country of northeast Mississippi. BLM GLO land patents document Chickasaw Cession entries. The county had a smaller enslaved population than Delta counties, with a yeoman farmer economy. William Faulkner drew on Ripley and Tippah County history for his novels. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the area. FamilySearch has digitized Tippah County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the late 1830s document early families. The Tippah County Historical Society maintains local indexes.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Tippah County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Ripley maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Tippah County Chickasaw Cession and Removal Records -- NARA / BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: NARA (Record Group 75) / Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tippah County was formed from Chickasaw Cession lands (Treaty of Pontotoc, 1832). BLM GLO records document federal land sales following removal. NARA Record Group 75 holds Chickasaw removal rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War Confederate service records for Tippah County soldiers; Mississippi contributed heavily to the Confederate army; MDAH holds state adjutant general records; NARA has compiled Confederate service records; Fold3 has digitized many; also includes WWI/WWII draft registrations
